turns.content: I've read about the validator crate and how it uses derive macros to attach validation attributes like Required or Length, but I still don't fully understand how these annotations actually connect to my existing schema types.

When you use the validator crate with a struct that derives Validatable, the library scans your fields for those specific attribute tokens. If a field has the Required annotation and its value is None at runtime, it will trigger a validation error before your data reaches downstream services like an API gateway or database layer.
That sounds straightforward, but I'm confused about how nested structures get validated automatically when I nest them in my main struct. Does validator just skip those inner types?

No, the crate doesn't skip them; it recursively validates every field including nested structs or enums. Think of it like a security checkpoint where you must pass through every single station on your route before exiting.

Right, but my main application uses a custom error type for reporting issues to users instead of the generic validator errors, so I'm wondering if I can still use these annotations without breaking my existing error handling flow.

You absolutely can. The validator crate provides an Into<YourErrorType> trait that allows you to convert its specific validation errors into your custom format while preserving the original field names and context.
